pois.name: Ohio Statehouse
pois.text: Sensors detect the Ohio Statehouse nearby — the local seat of planetary governance, Greek Revival class. Its dome was left unfinished for 68 years, proving all great projects have a task board.

pois.name: John Glenn International
pois.text: Passing John Glenn International — a spaceport named for a man who orbited Earth three times in a ship smaller than our cargo bay. Local craft are limited to the troposphere. Adorable.

pois.name: Buckeye Lake
pois.text: To starboard: Buckeye Lake, Ohio's oldest state park — a hand-dug canal reservoir from the 1820s. Early terraforming, Captain. Crude but heartfelt.

pois.name: Zanesville Y-Bridge
pois.text: Approaching Zanesville, home of the legendary Y-Bridge — one of the only bridges in the galaxy you can cross and still be on. Amelia Earhart used it as a landmark. So do we.

pois.name: New Concord — John Glenn's hometown
pois.text: Captain. All stations, attention. We are passing New Concord, Ohio — hometown of John Glenn: fighter pilot, first American to orbit the Earth, and at 77 the oldest human in space. This vessel dips its shields in respect. Godspeed, hometown of Godspeed.

pois.name: National Road & Zane Grey Museum
pois.text: Nearby: the National Road Museum. This very highway follows America's first federal road, built starting 1811 — the original hyperspace bypass, minus the Vogons. We travel in ancient wheel-ruts, Captain.

pois.name: Old Washington S-Bridge
pois.text: Historical anomaly nearby: an original S-shaped bridge of the old National Road. Engineers curved it to cross the creek square-on. Or to confuse oxen. Records differ. Science honors both theories.

pois.name: Cambridge — Glass City
pois.text: Passing Cambridge, Ohio — once a galactic capital of glassmaking. The cargo bay reports our own glassware rattling in what can only be professional respect.

pois.name: Blaine Hill Bridge
pois.text: Just below us: the Blaine Hill Bridge, built 1828 — the oldest bridge in Ohio, three sandstone arches still standing off the starboard bow. They built for the ages back then, Captain. So do we, in our way.

pois.name: Wheeling Suspension Bridge
pois.text: Final approach flourish: the Wheeling Suspension Bridge, dead ahead — when it opened in 1849 it was the longest suspension span on the planet. It carried the National Road across the Ohio River. Welcome to Wheeling, Captain.

pois.name: Oglebay Resort
pois.text: Long range detects Oglebay to the north — 2,000 acres of park, gardens, and a festival of lights visible from considerable altitude. Local terraforming at its most decorative.
